Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ7-CKP3-S6Y9-423D-00000-00Peter Thiel Panthalassa Investment Signals a Strange New Frontier for AI Power2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:56ZLexisNexisNEWSPeter Thiel Panthalassa Investment Signals a Strange New Frontier for AI Power

A company called Panthalassa has been discreetly constructing steel orbs intended to survive alone in the middle of the Pacific somewhere off the coast of Oregon, in a city more famous for its bridges and rain than for ocean technology. That silence ended last week. The concept of operating artificial intelligence on a floating buoy in the...

A company called Panthalassa has been discreetly constructing steel orbs intended to survive alone in the middle of the Pacific somewhere off the coast of Oregon, in a city more famous for its bridges and rain than for ocean technology. That silence ended ...

A company called Panthalassa has been discreetly constructing steel orbs intended to survive alone in the middle of the Pacific somewhere off the coast of Oregon, in a city more famous for its bridges and rain than for ocean technology. That silence ended last week. The concept of operating artificial intelligence on a floating buoy in the middle of nowhere ceased to sound like a pitch deck fantasy after Peter Thiel led a $140 million Series B investment in the business. It began to sound like something that investors would be prepared to lose a lot of money on. The idea is bizarre enough to warrant a second look. Panthalassa creates self- sufficient nodes that harness wave energy to produce clean electricity, which is then used to power onboard AI inference chips. Low-Earth-orbit satellites beam tokens back to Earth. The hardware is conveniently supercooled for free by the ocean. Somewhere in this story, it seems like someone asked, “What if data centers just floated?” in a meeting, and no one said no. Keys Values Company Panthalassa Headquarters Portland, Oregon Founded 2016 Co-Founder & CEO Garth Sheldon-Coulson Sector Renewable Energy / Ocean Technology / AI Infrastructure Latest Funding $140 million Series B Round Led By Peter Thiel Notable Investors John Doerr, Marc Benioff’s TIME Ventures, Max Levchin’s SciFi Ventures, Founders Fund, Lowercarbon Capital Core Product Autonomous floating nodes that generate wave power and run AI inference at sea Prototypes Deployed Ocean-1 (2021), Ocean-2 (2024) Next Milestone Ocean-3 deployment in the northern Pacific, 2026 Commercial Target 2027 Structure Public Benefit Corporation The announcement’s quote from Thiel sounds like classic Thiel. “We have no idea how much more computing will be needed in the future. These days, extraterrestrial solutions are not just science fiction. The ocean frontier has been opened by Panthalassa. Depending on the decade you’re in, this kind of statement can sound either visionary or ridiculous. It appears that investors think it’s the former. After all, the check is substantial. The company it keeps makes this round more intriguing than the majority of climate-flavored AI bets. It’s John Doerr. Susquehanna, Hanwha, Fortescue Ventures, Dylan Field, Super Micro, Max Levchin’s SciFi Ventures, Marc Benioff’s TIME Ventures, and a few returning believers like Founders Fund and Lowercarbon Capital are also doing this. Climate-focused funds are seated next to ardent tech contrarians at that oddly mixed table. Generally speaking, they don’t agree on much. On this, they are in agreement. Peter Thiel Panthalassa Investment Most likely, this is because the AI compute crunch is now a real, physical bottleneck rather than just a slide in a deck. Land-based data centers are facing grid constraints, cooling water shortages, disputes over permits with nearby communities, and supply chains that are just not moving quickly enough. The scope of the issue is not getting smaller, as anyone who has passed one of those gray, windowless buildings outside of Phoenix or Northern Virginia can attest. According to Panthalassa, no one lives in the ocean to complain about the noise, and the ocean contains more energy than the grid could ever supply. It was neatly framed by CEO Garth Sheldon-Coulson: open ocean, nuclear, and solar. There are three terawatt-scale potential sources. Both decades of engineering and decades of public skepticism have contributed to the first two. Not much has been done to the third. The company has been working on the issue since 2016. Ocean-1 and Ocean-2 were deployed in 2021 and 2024, respectively, and Ocean-3 is currently being prepared for the North Pacific later this year, with commercial deployments scheduled for 2027. The historical rhyme in this passage is difficult to ignore. In 2008, SpaceX sounded absurd. Tesla appeared to be doomed several times. Wave energy has a long history of failed startups, as many skeptics will point out, and they would be correct. The engineering that most of us will never see, in waters that most of us will never visit, will determine whether Panthalassa survives that cemetery. For the time being, however, the wager has been made, the funds are flowing, and a steel orb is being prepared for an extremely long and solitary shift somewhere off the Oregon coast. The post appeared first on ABC Money .

APPENDIX II STOCK IDENTIFIERS

SECTION 1 CORPORATE PROFILE

1.1 Activities

IDACORP, Inc., a holding company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, operates primarily through its subsidiary Idaho Power Company, focusing on the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of electricity in the Pacific Northwest region of the United States. The company's core business centers on delivering reliable electric services to residential, commercial, and industrial customers in Idaho and Oregon, emphasizing cost-effective energy solutions and infrastructure maintenance. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy initiatives, including wind and solar projects, which have expanded rapidly due to rising demand for sustainable power and supportive regulatory policies. This growth is further bolstered by investments in grid modernization and new customer connections, such as those supporting data centers and population increases, enabling IDACORP to enhance its market position in a competitive energy landscape. It is the NYSE's 16th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

Voya Financial Inc. (NYSE:VOYA) stands as a distinctive player in the financial sector, primarily focused on delivering comprehensive retirement savings solutions and workplace retirement plans. The firm is renowned for its Voya 401(k) plans, effectively positioning itself as a leading retirement plan provider. Voya Financial excels in managing employer-sponsored retirement plans, offering robust retirement plan administration, recordkeeping, and advisory services through its Voya Retirement Solutions division. Beyond traditional plans, such as individual retirement accounts (IRAs) and 403(b) plans, the company provides innovative 457 plans and annuities, including Voya annuities, target date funds, and managed accounts. Voya’s expertise in defined contribution plans and pension risk transfer highlights its commitment to plan sponsor services and automatic enrollment retirement plans. The company also leads in retirement plan compliance (ERISA compliance) and employee financial wellness initiatives, offering small business retirement solutions, retirement rollovers, retirement readiness tools, and top-tier investment management through Voya Investment Management, enhancing its competitive positioning in the marketplace.

SECTION 2 PAST YEAR PRESS RELEASES & PAST MONTH ECONOMIC NEWS

2.1 Press Releases and Corporate Wire

Press Release article 1 of 5, Source: WEC, 75 words

April 20: IDACORP quarterly dividends steady

IDACORP today announced a quarterly dividend of 88.0c per share for Q1/2026; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q4/2025) dividend of 88.0c. The ex-dividend date is Tuesday, May 05, 2026 and the record date is Tuesday, May 05, 2026 and it is payable on Monday, June 01. Total dividends per share paid in the 12 months ended April 20 were $3.48.

Press Release article 2 of 5, Source: Company Website, 275 words

February 19: IDACORP, Inc. Announces Fourth Quarter and Year-End 2025 Results, Initiates 2026 Earnings Guidance

BOISE, Idaho--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- IDACORP, Inc. (NYSE: IDA) reported fourth quarter 2025 net income attributable to IDACORP of $43.6 million, or $0.78 per diluted share, compared with $37.9 million, or $0.70 per diluted share, in the fourth quarter of 2024. For the full year ended December 31, 2025, IDACORP reported net income attributable to IDACORP of $323.5 million, or $5.90 per diluted share, compared with $289.2 million, or $5.50 per diluted share, in 2024.

"IDACORP's earnings in 2025 were at the top-end of the previously increased earnings guidance range. Results benefited from customer growth, rate changes, lower income tax expense, and the use of tax credits under the company's Idaho regulatory mechanism," said IDACORP President and Chief Executive Officer Lisa Grow. "Partially offsetting those benefits were higher depreciation and financing costs from infrastructure investments."

"We accomplished a great deal in 2025, from breaking ground on our Boardman-to-Hemingway transmission line to achieving record system reliability for our customers. As we move ahead, we are heavily focused on affordability and reliability for our customers as we continue to execute on our infrastructure development plans, with 2026 being another year of substantial capital investment for our company," Grow added.

IDACORP is initiating its full-year 2026 earnings guidance in the range of $6.25 to $6.45 per diluted share with the expectation that Idaho Power will use less than $30 million of additional tax credits available under the Idaho regulatory mechanism in 2026. The earnings guidance assumes normal weather conditions and power supply expenses throughout the year.

Source: Company Website

Press Release article 3 of 5, Source: Company Website, 301 words

February 05: Events Calendar: IDACORP Schedules Fourth Quarter 2025 Earnings Release & Conference Call

BOISE, Idaho--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- IDACORP, Inc. (NYSE:IDA) will report its fourth quarter results on Thursday, February 19, in a news release before the stock markets open. The company will hold an analyst conference call that day at 2:30 p.m. Mountain Time (4:30 p.m. Eastern Time) to discuss the fourth quarter 2025 earnings.

All parties interested in listening may do so through a live Webcast or by calling 855‑761‑5600 for listen-only mode. The passcode for the call is 9290150. The conference call logistics are posted on the company's website (www.idacorpinc.com) and will be included in the company's earnings news release. Slides will be included during the conference call. To access the slide deck, please visit www.idacorpinc.com/investor-relations. A replay of the conference call will be available on the company's website for a period of 12 months and will be available shortly after the call.

Background Information

IDACORP, Inc. (NYSE: IDA), Boise, Idaho-based and formed in 1998, is a holding company comprised of Idaho Power, a regulated electric utility; IDACORP Financial, an investor in affordable housing and other real estate tax credit investments; and Ida-West Energy, an operator of small hydroelectric generation projects that satisfy the requirements of the Public Utility Regulatory Policies Act of 1978. Idaho Power, headquartered in vibrant and fast-growing Boise, Idaho, has been a locally operated energy company since 1916. Today, it serves a 24,000-square-mile service area in Idaho and Oregon. With 17 low-cost hydropower projects at the core of its diverse energy mix, Idaho Power's residential, business, and agricultural customers pay among the nation's lowest prices for electricity. Its 2,100 employees proudly serve more than 660,000 customers with a culture of safety first, integrity always, and respect for all.

The ex-dividend date is Thursday, February 05, 2026 and the record date is Thursday, February 05, 2026 and it is payable on Monday, March 02. December 31 2025: IDACORP: File SEC Form 10-K - Management's Discussion and Analysis 10-K

MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS

In this MD&A section of this report, the general financial condition and results of operations for IDACORP and its subsidiaries and Idaho Power and its subsidiary are discussed. The discussion of IDACORP's and Idaho Power's general financial condition and results of operations for 2024 compared with 2023 can be found in their Annual Report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2024. See Part II - Item 7 - MD&A in that report for further information on the companies' prior period results of operations. While reading this MD&A, please refer to the accompanying consolidated financial statements of IDACORP and Idaho Power. Also refer to "Cautionary Note Regarding Forward-Looking Statements" and Part I - Item 1A - "Risk Factors" in this report for important information regarding forward-looking statements made in this MD&A section and elsewhere in this report.

INTRODUCTION

IDACORP is a holding company whose principal operating subsidiary is Idaho Power. IDACORP's common stock is listed and trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the trading symbol "IDA". Idaho Power is an electric utility whose rates and other matters are regulated by the IPUC, OPUC, and FERC. Idaho Power generates revenues and cash flows primarily from the sale and distribution of electricity to customers in its Idaho and Oregon service areas, as well as from the wholesale sale and transmission of electricity. On February 13, 2026, Idaho Power entered into a definitive agreement to sell its Oregon electric distribution business and associated distribution assets, as well as certain Oregon transmission assets, to OTEC. The closing of the transaction is subject to various conditions, including approvals of the OPUC, IPUC, and FERC.

To view more press releases from the past year, please access the link provided below.

http://www.buysellsignals.com/bst/01599196101050526772

2.2 ECONOMIC NEWS

ECO NEWS 1 of 1, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 275 words

Apr 13, 2026: Oregon Appeals Court Reverses PacifiCorp Wildfire Verdict, Directly Benefiting IDACORP Given Service Territory Overlap and Shared Regulatory Environment

On April 8, 2026, the Oregon Court of Appeals ruled in favor of Berkshire Hathaway Energy's PacifiCorp subsidiary in the James class-action case tied to Oregon's 2020 Labor Day wildfires. A three-judge appellate panel found that the trial court erred in instructing the jury that it could assume evidence presented at trial regarding four different wildfires applied to all class members. The ruling reverses and remands the case back to the trial court.

According to financial estimates, Berkshire has projected the cost could reach tens of billions of dollars, including $1.1 billion awarded in a series of already-completed trials. The decision could pause more than 160 damages trials scheduled through 2027. Separately, PacifiCorp has agreed to pay over $2 billion to settle claims, including $575 million to the federal government. Plaintiffs' counsel has called the ruling a procedural setback and may appeal to the Oregon Supreme Court.

Impact on IDACORP: IDA

Following this news, IDACORP faces a directly favorable development given its geographic and operational proximity to PacifiCorp. IDACORP's subsidiary, Idaho Power, serves customers in southern Idaho and eastern Oregon, regions that overlap with PacifiCorp's service territory and share similar wildfire risk profiles driven by dry climate conditions, mountainous terrain, and extensive power line infrastructure in fire-prone areas.

The Oregon appellate ruling's procedural standard is immediately applicable to any future wildfire litigation against Idaho Power in Oregon courts. Additionally, IDACORP has been expanding its wildfire mitigation investments, and the ruling validates the approach of defending individual claims on their specific facts rather than facing broad class-wide liability.

value.Title: California independence is already working

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHV-WYS3-S4W1-C3J8-00000-00California independence is already working2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:56ZLexisNexisUS NEWSCalifornia independence is already workingJoe Mathews, Zócalo Public Square

California independence is not a crazy idea.

California independence is an everyday reality.

And it’s working beautifully.

Just look at recent history. For decades, California has operated with levels of economic, regulatory, and moral autonomy that rival sovereign nations.

That autonomy is most visible on environmental matters. California has protections for endangered species and the coast that exceed American law. California has waivers from the federal Clean Air Act—which allowed it to fight smog and build its own highly skilled regulatory agencies.

The results are stunning. While the U.S. doubles down on coal and oil and gas, California’s power grid is increasingly powered by wind, solar and battery storage. Electric cars have proliferated, and greenhouse gas reduction targets for 2020 met. The state’s climate rules have touched off similar planning in subnational and local governments worldwide

California has set its own, far-reaching labor standards that protect gig workers and fast-food employees. The California Consumer Privacy Act governs how Silicon Valley treats data worldwide. And when the federal government repealed “net neutrality” rules, Californiaimposed its own law to prevent internet service providers from favoring specific content.

California is an essential independent force for human rights in an America turning against those rights. When the Supreme Court rolled back reproductive rights, California enshrined them in its constitution. In the face of Americans’ anti-immigrant hysteria, California extends rights in health, education, and mobility to all its residents, regardless of their legal status. And California’s sanctuary laws remain a declaration of independence from U.S. mass deportation policies that include enforced disappearances, concentration camps, and other crimes against humanity.

All this independence is why Trump aims attacks and lies against California. It’s harder for him to consolidate his dictatorship in the face of our independent successes.

We integrate immigrants into our communities, so he steals them away. We make progress finding housing for our unhoused people, so he freezes funding. We reduce greenhouse gases, so he cancels the federal “Endangerment Finding,” a legal basis for our climate fight.

We’re leaders AI research, so he terminates funding. Our economy runs on trade, so after California blocked his tariffs in the U.S. Supreme Court, Trump ignored the ruling and reimposed them.

He seeks to make California a colony from which he can extract money and resources. One example: his use of the Iran war to invoke the Defense Production Act, seize control of California’s energy infrastructure, and open a Santa Barbara oil pipeline, a blatant violation of our laws and constitution.

Frighteningly, some candidates for governor—Republicans Steve Hilton and Chad Bianco, and Democrat Matt Mahan—argue for trading in independence for a “normal relationship” with the U.S. regime.

That’s nuts. Surrendering independence would be suicidal, and not just because independence is California’s best survival mechanism. Surrendering independence would also be bad for Americans—since California independence is a buffer against U.S. government volatility.

When the federal government shuts down because of partisan gridlock, California’s government stays open. When the president spikes gas prices, California’s renewable energy investments provide alternatives. When Trump issues destructive economic orders on taxes or spending, capital can flow to steady, growing, innovative California.

In short, California’s independence is not a risky gambit. It’s a safety measure, a force for stability, here at home and around the globe.

America, we are your Western Firewall.

You’re welcome.

And with the U.S. turning against California values—democracy, freedom, entrepreneurialism, diversity, inclusion—now is the moment to become even more independent.

California, often in cooperation with Oregon and Washington, is already replacing the federal agencies in public health, environment, and science that Trump dismantled. Our next step should be to create our own IRS and Federal Reserve, to preserve independent banking and tax enforcement. And we should boost our own civil defense, as Trump’s stupid wars degrade the U.S. military.

Then California can become a fully independent republic—and the world’s most successful country.

Joe Mathews writes the Connecting California column for Zócalo Public Square.

This article originally appeared on Ventura County Star: California independence is already working

value.Title: Angus King III for Maine governor | Letter

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJK-DGM3-RRHW-H3TV-00000-00Angus King III for Maine governor | Letter2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:56ZLexisNexisAngus King III for Maine governor | Letter

I am a supporter of Angus King III for governor because of his stance on climate change and how he intends to use renewable energy to decrease the power cost escalation we are seeing in Maine.

Clean energy has proven to be the lowest-cost way to enlarge energy supply, increase reliability and reduce carbon emissions throughout the globe. Unfortunately, key renewable energy projects have been delayed here in Maine, as well as across the country, through a number of avenues. Politically motivated attacks by the federal government, as well as red tape locally, have increased the costs of developing renewable energy projects - costs that are then passed on to the citizens of Maine.

Maine currently has the third fastest utility rate growth in the country. As governor, King III will champion lower power costs, increased reliability and cleaner energy.

Harry Ostrander, LEED AP
Portland

value.Title: Angus King III for Maine governor | Letter

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJK-DGM3-RRHW-H3TP-00000-00Angus King III for Maine governor | Letter2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:56ZLexisNexisAngus King III for Maine governor | Letter

I am a supporter of Angus King III for governor because of his stance on climate change and how he intends to use renewable energy to decrease the power cost escalation we are seeing in Maine.

Clean energy has proven to be the lowest-cost way to enlarge energy supply, increase reliability and reduce carbon emissions throughout the globe. Unfortunately, key renewable energy projects have been delayed here in Maine, as well as across the country, through a number of avenues. Politically motivated attacks by the federal government, as well as red tape locally, have increased the costs of developing renewable energy projects - costs that are then passed on to the citizens of Maine.

Maine currently has the third fastest utility rate growth in the country. As governor, King III will champion lower power costs, increased reliability and cleaner energy.

Harry Ostrander, LEED AP
Portland

value.Title: Toyota Tsusho Corp Sees 8% Rise in Profits Amid Global Economic Uncertainties
value.Overview: Global Round Up - ADRs and Depository Receipts, 668words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-YV13-SHFW-V55M-00000-00Toyota Tsusho Corp Sees 8% Rise in Profits Amid Global Economic Uncertainties2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:56ZLexisNexisToyota Tsusho Corp Sees 8% Rise in Profits Amid Global Economic Uncertainties

DAILY ADR AND DEPOSITORY RECEIPT REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

06 May 2026 09:15 EDT

Toyota Tsusho Corp has reported an 8 percent increase in earnings attributable to shareholders for fiscal year 2025, reflecting a robust 12 percent rise in revenue compared to the previous year. The financial report, released on 4th May 2026, showcases the company's resilience in the automotive and metals sectors amid a backdrop of global economic uncertainties.

The fiscal year for Toyota Tsusho ended on 31st March 2026. During this period, the company expanded its footprint in the recycling industry by acquiring Radius Recycling, a Portland, Oregon-based firm formerly known as Schnitzer Steel Industries. This acquisition has enabled Toyota Tsusho to enhance its network of metal recycling facilities across the United States and Canada, as well as a recycled-content electric arc furnace steel mill located in Portland.

Toyota Tsusho anticipates significant synergies from the Radius Recycling acquisition, focusing on three key areas: metal scrap, end-of-life vehicles, and automotive batteries. The company believes these synergies will bolster its growth in circular economy initiatives and accelerate its efforts toward achieving carbon neutrality. Additionally, the firm and its subsidiary, Toyota Tsusho America Inc. (TAI), already had a presence in North America through its Green Metals Inc. network, which includes more than a dozen recycling facilities and various partnerships.

Despite the challenges posed by trade policy uncertainties—especially new tariffs imposed by the U.S.—the overall global economy remained stable during the fiscal year. However, Toyota Tsusho noted that geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East and Ukraine could complicate the outlook for fiscal year 2026, increasing risks related to energy supplies and broader market conditions.

Within the earnings report, Toyota Tsusho highlighted a slight decline in earnings from its Circular Economy business unit, which fell by 4.5 percent year-on-year, attributed mainly to one-time factors. Similarly, the Metal+(Plus) unit saw a marginal decrease of 0.7 percent. Nevertheless, the company expressed optimism about the future, citing rising resource market prices as a positive factor for its ongoing operations.

IN TODAY'S REPORT:

SECTION 1 TOYOTA TSUSHO ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 Toyota Tsusho Activities

Activities

Toyota Tsusho Corporation, a prominent member of the Toyota Group and a global trading company headquartered in Japan, specializes in a diverse range of core business activities. These include trading and distribution of metals, machinery, automotive parts, energy resources, chemicals, and consumer goods, alongside investments in logistics, infrastructure projects, and sustainable initiatives. The company operates through key segments such as Global Parts & Logistics, Metals, and Machinery & Electronics, facilitating international supply chains and business development across more than 80 countries. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy solutions, including solar and wind power projects, driven by global decarbonization efforts, as well as electric vehicle components and advanced materials, capitalizing on the automotive industry's shift toward electrification. Additionally, Toyota Tsusho is expanding in food and agriculture, leveraging digital technologies for enhanced efficiency and market reach, positioning it as a resilient player in the evolving global economy. It is the International Depository Receipt (IDR) market's 5th largest Machinery company by market capitalisation.

value.Title: Building the future Trump and his goons can't destroy

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGS-R7X3-RVY7-P4N3-00000-00Building the future Trump and his goons can't destroy2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:06:21ZLexisNexisBuilding the future Trump and his goons can't destroy

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

May 3rd, 2026 (Alternet — Delivered by Newstex)

I'm not going to dwell on this week's outrages by Trump and his regime — his Supreme Court appointees gutting the Voting Rights Act, his bizarre claim that his war doesn't require congressional approval because it's #8216;terminated,#8217; his murders of additional sailors he suspects of smuggling drugs, his ongoing ICE raids, his continuing efforts to use the Justice Department to punish perceived enemies, his renewed attempts to silence comedians such as Jimmy Kimmel, his giant self-aggrandizing triumphal arch (what triumph?) and grotesque ballroom, his never-ending corruption and lies.

Yet in the midst of this cataclysm, the backlash against Trump is growing. Your work has been critical — your demonstrations and No Kings marches, your letters and phone calls to Congress, your boycotts and your local activism. Trump's approval ratings are in the cellar. Two-thirds oppose his war. Many are demanding an end to his reign. Even some MAGA faithful are turning on him.

But it's more than resisting Trump.

A new progressivism is being born.

Many of you are leading this. You are the activists in Montana and Hawaii pushing a new vision for reining in corporate power. You're the activists in California and New York devising new ways to tax the super-wealthy to pay for what most people need.

You're the activists in Massachusetts taking the lead on reproductive rights and access to health care. In Washington state, pushing new and more aggressive environmental regulations and renewable energy. In San Diego, Oakland, and San Francisco, devising new ways to address homelessness. Organizations like Voz in Portland, Oregon, empowering immigrant day laborers.

You are the activists in red states refusing to let warehouses be turned into migrant detention centers or let their towns host giant AI data centers.

You're raising the minimum wage across America. Since the start of this year, 22 states have raised it, bringing the total number of jurisdictions raising the minimum — including cities and counties — to almost 90, with 18 states and the District of Columbia now mandating $15 an hour or more.

You're supporting a new generation of young progressives in Congress: Maxwell Frost, Summer Lee, Greg Casar, Delia Ramirez, and the just-elected Analilia Mejia. And new progressive local leaders like New York's mayor Zohran Mamdani.

You're encouraging a new cohort of progressive rising stars, including Minneapolis's Omar Fateh, Tennessee's Aftyn Behn, Wisconsin's Francesca Hong, Illinois's Kat Abughazaleh, Texas's James Talarico, San Francisco's Saikat Chakrabarti, Maine's Graham Platner, Tennessee's Justin Pearson, Michigan's Abdul El-Sayed, Florida's Elijah Manley, and many others.

In my six decades in and around American politics, I've never seen anything close to the progressive talent and energy that's now emerging.

Make no mistake. These are terrible times — the worst I've lived through, and I've lived through some bad ones. (Remember 1968? Nixon's enemies list? Anyone old enough to recall Joe McCarthy's communist witch hunts?) Trump and his goons are doing everything they can to destroy America and much of the world.

But out of the embers and rubble that Trump and his despicable regime have wrought, a new America is being born. We are making it happen.

As long as we are alive and as long as we are resolved, as long as we are taking action to stop the worst of this catastrophe and also trying to make America and the world better, have no doubt: We will prevail.

Be safe. Hug your loved ones. Never, ever give up.

Robert Reich is a professor of public policy at Berkeley and former secretary of labor. His writings can be found at https://robertreich.substack.com
